summary: No-ship: the daemon can orphan listener processes on shutdown and can double-spawn listener jobs after an exit/restart cycle; listener jobs are also still eligible for cron execution in `tick()`, which breaks the one-worker-per-listener contract.

findings.title: Listener jobs are still executed by the cron tick path, so a `type:"listener"` job can run both as a supervised daemon worker and as a normal scheduled job
findings.recommendation: Exclude `job.type === "listener"` from `tick()`, or otherwise separate listener definitions from cron-fired jobs. As written, any listener with a valid cron expression can be spawned once by `superviseListeners()` and again by `tick()`, violating the acceptance requirement of one worker per `type:listener` job and risking duplicate side effects.

findings.title: Listener restart timers are not tracked or cancelled, allowing double-spawn after a listener exits
findings.recommendation: Track per-listener restart timers and clear them when a replacement worker is started or during shutdown. Also guard `spawnListener()` against an existing live worker for the same `job.id`. Today, after `exit`, the code schedules `setTimeout(() => spawnListener(job,...))`; if `superviseListeners()` runs before that timer fires, it spawns a new worker immediately, and the pending timer later spawns a second one.

findings.title: Graceful shutdown exits the parent after 100ms without waiting for listener children to terminate, which can leave orphaned workers running
findings.recommendation: Wait for child exit events (with a bounded timeout, then escalate if needed) before `process.exit(0)`. The current handler sends `SIGTERM` and unconditionally exits 100ms later, so long-lived listeners can survive as detached/orphaned processes, violating the graceful shutdown requirement and leaking resources.
